coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots with bright fluorescence for highly sensitive detection of Fe3+ in environmental waters
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Yan
Chen, Guoqing
Ma, Chaoqun
Gu, Jiao
Yang, Taiqun
Li, Lei
Gao, Hui
Xiong, Yi
Wu, Yamin

Wu, Hui
Yin, Wenzhi
Hu, Anqi
Qiu, Xiaoqian
Guan, Weinan
Zhang, Wei
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we synthesized stable nitrogen-doped carbon dots by a simple and economical one-step hydrothermal method using l-cysteine and anhydrous ethylenediamine as precursors. The prepared carbon dots have bright and stable blue light emission near 383 nm and can be used as fluorescent probes to detect the concentration of Fe3+ in environmental waters. It was demonstrated that due to intermolecular electrostatic interaction, a non-fluorescent complex N-CDs/Fe3+ is formed by coordination of Fe3+ with amino and carboxyl functional groups on the surface of carbon dots. Therefore, in combination with internal filtration effect, the fluorescence of carbon dots can be quenched in the presence of Fe3+, and the degree of quenching is linearly related to the concentration of Fe3+. The limit of detection in deionized water was as low as 0.069 μM with R2 of 0.998 and a linear range of 0.3 to 20 μM. In addition, satisfactory recoveries were achieved for the determination of Fe3+ in environmental water samples. The method is reliable, with highly sensitivity and selectivity, and has potential for practical applications in environmental metal analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent probe
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Fe3+ ion detection
Internal filtration effect
Static quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of detection of Fe3+
Detection of Fe3+ in real water samples

Selectivity and sensitivity of detection of Fe3+
Detection of Fe3+ in real water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM image of N-CDs at 10 nm; (b) Size distribution of N-CDs; (c) XRD image of N-CDs; (d) FTIR spectrum of N-CDs.
(a) XPS spectra of N-CDs and high resolution peaks of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s and (e) S2p.

(a) Spectra of N-CDs including UV–vis absorption (black line), fluorescence excitation (red line) and emission spectra (blue line). (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths.

(a) Relative fluorescence intensity after incorporation of different metal ions of as-synthesized N-CDs; (b) The effect of Fe3+ on the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the presence of other metal ions.

When the Fe3+ concentration was 0–100 μM, (a) fluorescence spectra of the mixed system under excitation at 315 nm; (b) the relationship between the quenching degree (F0-F)/F0 and the concentration of Fe3+; the inset showed the linear fitting curve between the degree of quenching and Fe3+ concentration when the Fe3+ concentration was 0.3–20 μM.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the mixed system at 315 nm for Fe3+ concentrations of 0–60 μM in tap water samples; (b) The degree of quenching (F0-F)/F0 versus Fe3+ concentration of 0–60 μM in tap water samples; the inset showed the linear fit curve between the degree of quenching and Fe3+ concentration for Fe3+ concentrations of 1–30 μM. (c) Fluorescence spectra of the mixed system under the excitation at 315 nm in the lake water sample when the Fe3+ concentration was 0–150 μM; (d) The relationship between the degree of quenching (F0-F)/F0 and Fe3+ concentration in the lake water sample when the Fe3+ concentration was 0–150 μM; the inset showed the linear fitting curve between the degree of quenching and Fe3+ concentration when the Fe3+ concentration was 2–30 μM.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of Fe3+, fluorescence excitation spectra and emission spectra of N-CDs; (b) Fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs solution before and after adding Fe3+.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra before and after the reaction of N-CDs with Fe3+; (b) FTIR spectra of N-CDs solution after adding Fe3+; (c) XPS spectra of N-CDs after adding Fe3+; (d) Zeta potential of N-CDs and N-CDs/Fe3+.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of N-CDs and Fe3+detection.
Comparison of detection strategies and limits of detection with some of the currently published literature for Fe3+ detection.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of the determination of Fe3+ ions in real samples using N-CDs fluorescent probes (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots with bright fluorescence for highly sensitive detection of Fe3+ in environmental waters

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Provincial Research Center of Light Industrial Optoelectronic Engineering and Technology, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Provincial Research Center of Light Industrial Optoelectronic Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Provincial Research Center of Light Industrial Optoelectronic Engineering and Technology, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Science, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we synthesized stable nitrogen-doped carbon dots by a simple and economical one-step hydrothermal method using l-cysteine and anhydrous ethylenediamine as precursors. The prepared carbon dots have bright and stable blue light emission near 383 nm and can be used as fluorescent probes to detect the concentration of Fe3+ in environmental waters. It was demonstrated that due to intermolecular electrostatic interaction, a non-fluorescent complex N-CDs/Fe3+ is formed by coordination of Fe3+ with amino and carboxyl functional groups on the surface of carbon dots. Therefore, in combination with internal filtration effect, the fluorescence of carbon dots can be quenched in the presence of Fe3+, and the degree of quenching is linearly related to the concentration of Fe3+. The limit of detection in deionized water was as low as 0.069 μM with R2 of 0.998 and a linear range of 0.3 to 20 μM. In addition, satisfactory recoveries were achieved for the determination of Fe3+ in environmental water samples. The method is reliable, with highly sensitivity and selectivity, and has potential for practical applications in environmental metal analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron is one of the essential trace elements and one of the main components of hemoglobin and hemoglobin in the human body, therefore almost all tissues in the human body contain iron. Trivalent iron ions (Fe3+) play a big part in maintaining normal physiological activities in the human body [1], such as the exchange and organization of respiratory processes, antibody production, lipid transport in the blood, intracellular detoxification of drugs, oxygen binding and transport, cell proliferation and differentiation, DNA and RNA synthesis, and gene regulation [2–6], among others. However, insufficient or excessive Fe3+ can cause various diseases, such as iron deficiency can lead to anemia [7], decreased immunity [8], etc. Excessive iron storage in the body can induce enhanced lipid peroxidation reactions, leading to imbalance in the body's oxidative and antioxidant systems, directly damaging DNA, inducing mutations, and being associated with tumors in various organs such as liver, lung, and esophagus [9–11]. One of the reasons for the abnormal Fe3+ content in human body is the possible existence of metal ions in drinking water that exceed the standard. Water is the basic guarantee to maintain normal human life activities, and metal contamination into the water cycle can directly cause great damage to human body. Consequently, the highly sensitive detection of Fe3+ in water is very necessary in terms of both environmental and biological systems.

Traditional methods for detecting Fe3+ include atomic emission spectrometry [12], atomic absorption spectrometry [13], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [14], and electrochemical methods [15], etc. Although these methods can detect the Fe3+ content accurately, the instruments used are expensive, the detection conditions are demanding, and the sample pre-treatment is cumbersome, which is not universally applicable to field detection. Carbon quantum dots are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with remarkable fluorescence properties, which consist of ultrafine, dispersed, quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with sizes less than 10 nm. Carbon quantum dots have excellent optical characteristics such as low toxicity, environmental friendliness, good water solubility and biocompatibility, and wide source of raw materials [16–20]. These advantages make carbon quantum dots promising nanomaterials for various fields such as biosensing, imaging, clinical diagnosis and disease treatment [21–23].

In this paper, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were successfully synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method with l-cysteine and anhydrous ethylenediamine as raw materials and water as the solvent. At an excitation wavelength of 315 nm N-CDs had intense blue light emission at around 383 nm. Based on the principle that Fe3+ can interact with a large number of amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of N-CDs to form N-CDs/Fe3+ non-fluorescent complexes and the internal filtration effect leading to the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs, a method was developed for sensitive detection of Fe3+ in tap water and lake water using fluorescence intensity variations (Scheme 1 ).

l-Cysteine (Cys) was purchased from Aamas-beta Reagent Co., ltd. Anhydrous ethylenediamine (EDA), CH3OH, CH2Cl2, silica gel (Acuity FCP. 300–400 mesh), NaOH, HCl, FeCl3·6H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, PbCl2, CaCl2, FeCl2·4H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, NaCl, KCl, CuCl2·2H2O, MgCl2, ZnCl2, CrCl3·6H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, Hg(NO3)2·H2O and AgNO3 were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. All chemical reagents were analytical reagent. The deionized water (18.2 MΩ-cm) used for experiments was prepared by Labonova Smart ultrapure water system from Think-lab, Germany.

All fluorescence spectra in the experiment were measured by FS5 fluorescence spectrometer which was from Edinburgh Instruments, UK. Morphological and lattice information of N-CDs were characterized by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images taken by a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope from Japan. All absorption spectra were obtained using a UV-2600 ultra-violet spectrophotometer which was manufactured in Japan. Frontier infrared spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA) recorded Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of N-CDs were recorded by ESCALAB 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer from USA for analysis of elemental composition. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) maps were presented with Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer. The fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs were measured on FLS920 steady-state/transient-state fluorescence spectrometer from Edinburgh Instruments, UK. By using Zetasizer Nano ZS90 nanoparticle size potentiometer from Malvern Instruments ltd., UK, Zeta potential was investigated.

N-CDs were prepareded by a one-step hydrothermal method. 0.8 g of l-cysteine and 4 mL of anhydrous ethylenediamine were mixed and ultrapure water was added until the final volume of the solution reached 40 mL. After being thoroughly stirred, the mixture was transferred into an autoclave containing 100 mL of tetrafluoroethylene liner and heated in an electric oven at 210 °C for 10 h. After completion of heating the reaction product was cooled naturally to room temperature. Subsequently, N-CDs were separated from impurities using silica gel column chromatography with a methanol/dichloromethane (1:8, V/V) mixture as the eluent. Finally, the N-CDs were concentrated using a rotary evaporator and the organic solvent was replaced with ultrapure water. After preparation, N-CDs solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for subsequent use.

The original solution of N-CDs was diluted to different multiples (10, 40, 70, 100, 150, 200 times), and then mixed with water and Fe3+ of the same concentration at a volume ratio of 1:1, respectively, and the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution was recorded to determine the optimal dilution multiple for the detection of Fe3+. N-CDs were mixed with equal concentrations of Fe3+ at volume ratios of 4:1, 3:1, 2:1, 1:1, 1:2, 1:3, and 1:4, respectively, and the fluorescence intensities of the mixed solutions were measured to determine the optimal volume ratio for detecting Fe3+. To investigate the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs and to find the optimal pH value with the greatest effect on quenching, N-CDs were diluted to the desired multiple with NaOH-HCl buffers (pH = 4,5,6,7,8,9,10), mixed with deionized water or Fe3+ at the optimal volume ratio and the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solutions was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of detection of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: First, we selected 14 kinds of common interfering cations (Ba2+、K+、Na+ 、Mg2+、Zn2+、Co2+、Ca2+、Fe2+、Cr3+、Mn2+、Pb2+、Ag+、Cu2+、Hg2+) to study the effect of these metal ions on the fluorescence of N-CDs to verify the selectivity of N-CDs to Fe3+. 100 μM Fe3+ or other cations were incubated with the same concentration of N-CDs at room temperature. Then we measured the fluorescence spectrum of the solution and recorded the fluorescence intensity at 383 nm. Then 1 mL of N-CDs solution diluted 100 times by NaOH-HCl buffer (pH = 8), was taken, to which 2 mL of Fe3+ solutions at varying concentrations were added, and the final solution volume was adjusted to 3 mL. The above samples were examined by fluorescence spectroscopy, wherein the excitation wavelength was 315 nm, the emission wavelength scanning range was 330–530 nm, and the interval was 1 nm. All experiments were repeated three times. The formula for calculating limit of detection was LOD = 3σ/s with σ of the standard deviation of 3 blank samples and s of the slope of the standard curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of this detection method in practice, the standard fortifying approach was used to detect Fe3+ in real water samples including tap water and lake water. Tap water samples were from our laboratory while lake water from the lake of our university. Both water samples were filtered by 0.22 µm microporous membranes before being put into the experiment. All analytical methods are consistent with those described in Section 2.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structural characterization of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the morphology and size of N-CDs, the particle morphology and particle size distribution of N-CDs were measured using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). As shown in Fig. 1 (a) for the HRTEM image at the 10 nm scale, the image showed that the prepared carbon dots were nearly spherical with good dispersion, ranging in size from 1.67 to 4.02 nm with an average diameter of 2.86 nm (Fig. 1b). XRD pattern (Fig. 1c) showed a strong diffraction peak at 2θ = 26.54°, corresponding to the (002) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon [24,25]. This is a typical carbon dot peak, indicating that the carbon structure is highly disordered and the carbon dots have relatively satisfactory crystallization properties [26]. Additionally, the diffraction peak at 2θ = 40.18° belongs to the C(100) plane [27]. The functional group structure on the surface of N-CDs was characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). As shown in Fig. 1(d), the peaks at high wave numbers of 3447 cm−1 and 2925 cm−1 correspond to the OH /NH stretching vibration and CH stretching vibration of N-CDs. The sharp absorption peak at 1644 cm−1 wave number corresponds to the asymmetric stretching vibration of CO in the carboxyl group. The absorption peak at 1322 cm−1 is attributed to CN, and the peak at 1092 cm−1 can be attributed to CO, CN. The characteristic peaks at 1034 cm−1 and 719 cm−1 confirm the presence of CS bond and SO bond [28]. Additionally, the absorption peak at the low wave number of 779 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of NH. The FTIR result indicates that the presence of a large amount of groups like –NH2 and –COOH on the N-CDs surface is favorable for the binding of Fe3+ to N-CDs.

To obtain more detailed and accurate information about the elemental composition and chemical structure of the N-CDs, XPS measurements spectra were carried out. The characteristic binding energy signals of C1s, N1s, O1s, and S2p exhibit four main peaks at 284.7 eV, 399.4 eV, 531.7 eV, and 150.5 eV (Fig. 2 a), with 59 %, 6.5 %, 27.15 %, and 7.35 % of each element, respectively. The spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2b) is divided into three peaks, the peak at 284.8 eV corresponds to sp2C (CC), and the peaks at 285.87 eV and 288.27 eV correspond to CN/CO and O-CO, respectively. The spectrum of N1s (Fig. 2c) has two peaks at 399.41 eV and 400.10 eV, respectively confirm the presence of CN and NH. The high-resolution energy spectrum of O1s (Fig. 2d) can be fitted to three peaks, namely CO at 531.87 eV, CO at 535.59 eV and OH at 530.88 eV. The high-resolution energy spectrum of S2p was peak fitted to obtain the results shown in Fig. 2(e). The two peaks located at 167.17 eV and 168.47 eV belong to S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 in the C-SOX group, while the two peaks located at 163.93 eV and 165.03 eV belong to S2p1/2 and S2p3/2 in the CS bond [29]. The characterization results shown by XPS spectra and FTIR spectra can be corroborated with each other.

The UV–vis absorption spectra and the fluorescence optimum excitation and peak wavelengths of N-CDs were measured to investigate the optical properties of N-CDs. The UV–vis absorption spectra (black line in Fig. 3 a) shows that in the range of 275–350 nm, N-CDs have broad absorption peaks which may be related to the n-π* jump [30]. Moreover, comparing the absorption spectra of N-CDs with those of the raw materials Cys and EDA (Fig. S1), it is evident that new absorption peaks appear in the spectra of N-CDs. The change can also prove the synthesis of the new substance N-CDs and the occurrence of the absorption peak is precisely related to the surface state of the carbon dots. The excitation spectrum of N-CDs (red line) shows the optimal excitation wavelength of 315 nm, and the peak wavelength of N-CDs (blue line) at this wavelength excitation is 383 nm. Additionally, the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs were measured at different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 3b). There was no significant change in the position of the emission peak as the excitation wavelength increased from 295 nm to 340 nm, indicating excitation-independent emission and only one emission state at the carbon dots [31].

Fluorescence stability under different conditions is important for using N-CDs as probes for detection in practice, so the fluorescence stability of N-CDs in NaCl solutions with different concentrations, the effect of time (days) on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs, and the resistance of N-CDs to photobleaching were studied. As shown in Figs. S2 (a) and (b), the fluorescence intensity remained nearly stable when the NaCl concentration was increased from 0 to 2.0 mol/L, indicating that the prepared carbon dots had excellent stability in the high concentration salt environment. Then, the N-CDs were incubated for one month, and the fluorescence emission were measured at an interval of every-five days. Fig. S2 (c) showed that the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs only started to show a slight decreasing trend after 20 days of incubation and did not change much throughout the incubation period. Finally, we continuously irradiated the N-CDs under a UV lamp with excitation wavelength of 365 nm for 120 min and measured the fluorescence intensity every 10 min, and the obtained results were shown in Fig. S2 (d). The fluorescence intensity remained relatively stable during the 120 min, although there were slight fluctuations. The above results indicate that the stability in salt solution and resistance to photobleaching of N-CDs make them an excellent choice for establishing fluorescence sensing strategies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of detection of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: For making the assay more sensitive and accurate, some experimental conditions were optimized, including N-CDs concentration, the volume ratio of N-CDs to Fe3+ in the working solution and the pH value (Fig. S3). Then, we measured the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs mixed with 15 common metal ions to verify the selectivity of N-CDs for Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), when N-CDs were incubated with a variety of metal ions of the same concentration respectively, among them, although individual ions also had a slight effect on the carbon dots, the comparison showed that the fluorescence burst effect of Fe3+ on the carbon dots was the most obvious. Moreover, Fig. 4(b) showed that when other metal ions coexisted with Fe3+, Fe3+ could still effectively quench the fluorescence of CDs. The degree of quenching was almost independent of other metal ions, indicating that the carbon dots had a relatively highly selectivity for Fe3+ ions.

Next, to evaluate the sensitivity of this strategy, we tested for Fe3+ in water using N-CDs fluorescent probes under optimal conditions. As Fe3+concentration in the system increased (0–100 μM), the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution at 315 nm excitation wavelength (Fig. 5 a) gradually decreased and the degree of quenching (Fig. 5b) showed a trend of rapid increase followed by a slow increase. When the Fe3+ concentration was 100 μM, the quenching degree reached 80 %. There was an excellent linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs and Fe3+ concentration with the added Fe3+ concentration of 0.3–20 μM (inset of Fig. 5b). By fitting this linear relationship, the equation obtained was (F0-F)/F0 = 0.02782[C] + 0.0282, where F0 and F are the peak intensities in the absence and presence of Fe3+ in the mixed solution, respectively and [C] is Fe3+ concentration. R2 was 0.998 and the calculated LOD was 0.069 μM. The results were compared with those in some of the currently published literature in Table 1 . The comparative results indicated that our assay had a relatively large advantage in sensitivity compared to other methods with lower than most fluorescence methods and was able to reach the same level as the lowest level currently available.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water and lake water were used as real samples to analyze the feasibility of N-CDs fluorescent probes for Fe3+ detection in real environments. The linear equation in section 3.3 was no longer applicable to practical applications because of the different interferences in different real samples. It was measured that there was no Fe3+ present in the original solution, so the original solution was used as solvent to which Fe3+ were added separately to configure a series of concentrations of standard samples. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the N-CDs were mixed with the actual samples to be tested. The fluorescence intensity and the degree of quenching of the mixed solutions in relation to the Fe3+ concentration were measured, and the results obtained were shown in Fig. 6 . The images indicated that the phenomenon in deionized water could also occur in real samples, and Fe3+ could still cause the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs. However, the specific linear range and detection limits obtained varied depending on the sample. As shown in Fig. 6(a) and 6(b), in tap water, 60 μM Fe3+ solution could cause about 80 % fluorescence burst of N-CDs. There was a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs and Fe3+ concentration with the added Fe3+ concentration of 1–30 μM (inset of Fig. 6b). By fitting this linear relationship, the equation obtained was (F0-F)/F0 = 0.01849[C] + 0.06783 with R2 of 0.997, and the calculated LOD was 0.21 μM. As shown in Fig. 6(c) and 6(d), when the lake water was used as the actual material to be measured, the presence of certain interfering factors in the lake samples inhibited the effect of Fe3+ causing fluorescence quenching, resulting in 150 μM of Fe3+ solution to cause about 60 % fluorescence quenching of N-CDs. However, fitting a linear relationship in the range of 2–30 μM still obtained the equation (F0-F)/F0 = 0.04544[C] + 0.01466 with R2 of 0.965 and LOD of 0.59 μM. Moreover, as shown in Table 2 , we performed recovery tests on the actual water samples using the standard addition method with recoveries in the range of 96.6 %-103.3 %. The relative standard deviations of all three measurements were below 3 %, which proved the feasibility of the scheme in practical applications.

The mechanism of the reaction between N-CDs and Fe3+ leading to fluorescence quenching was studied. First, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Fe3+ was measured and compared with the fluorescence spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 7 a). It was found that the absorption band of Fe3 + in the wavelength range of 260–400 nm had a large part overlap with the excitation peak of N-CDs at 315 nm and a small part overlap with the emission peak at 383 nm. This type of spectral overlap suggested that the fluorescence of carbon dots might be quenched by Fe3+ due to internal filtration effect [39] or fluorescence resonance energy transfer [40]. However, the fluorescence lifetime had to be measured to discriminate it and it was the most accurate and convenient method to identify static quenching from dynamic quenching [41]. Therefore, as shown in Fig. 7(b), the lifetime of N-CDs was measured in the absence and presence of Fe3+. The fluorescence lifetime decreased from 4.92 ns to 4.82 ns with negligible change after the interaction with 50 μM of Fe3+. While the fluorescence resonance energy transfer would significantly change the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs, therefore, one of the reasons why Fe3+ could cause the fluorescence quneching of N-CDs was that an internal filtration effect occurs between the two.

Next, UV–vis absorption spectrum before and after the reaction of N-CDs with Fe3+ were measured (Fig. 8 a). It was found that the spectrum did not show a new absorption peak, and the absorbance of the N-CDs/Fe3+ solution was not a mere summation of the N-CDs and Fe3+ absorbance values, but an enhancement on top of that. Such a change in the absorption spectrum might be attributed to the formation of non-fluorescent N-CDs/Fe3+ ground state complexes in the system, which underwent static quenching. Comparing the FTIR spectra of the N-CDs solution after adding Fe3+ (Fig. 8b) with that before the addition of Fe3+ (Fig. 1d), it could be observed that the intensity and peak patterns of the NH, OH and CO groups changed, and there was a slight shift in the position of the wave numbers where they were located. This might be attributed to the coordination of Fe3+ with the amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of N-CDs because of their strong affinity to them, forming N-CDs/Fe3+ non-fluorescent complexes [6]. The changes in the XPS spectra and the content of each element of the N-CDs solution after adding Fe3+ shown in Fig. 8(c) and Table S1 also proved that Fe3+ interacted with N-CDs, leading to changes in the N-CDs surface groups [42].The results of the zeta potential test (Fig. 8d) indicated that the zeta potential of the N-CDs solution was −35.9 mV, in contrast to −2.6 mV for the N-CDs/Fe3+ system, suggesting that Fe3+ and N-CDs surface groups might be coordinated due to intermolecular electrostatic interactions [43]. In conclusion, the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by Fe3+ was caused by a combination of the internal filtration effect and the formation of N-CDs/Fe3+ non-fluorescent complexes.

In this work, we successfully synthesized luminescence-stabilized nitrogen-doped carbon dots by a green and facile one-step hydrothermal method. Because of the internal filtration effect and intermolecular electrostatic interaction, the fluorescence of N-CDs was significantly quenched by Fe3+. Based on the above principle, the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs and Fe3+ concentration was obtained with the LOD as low as 0.069 μM, which achieved the purpose of efficient determination of Fe3+ concentration. Moreover, the LOD of 0.21 μM and linear range of 1–30 μM in tap water samples and 0.59 μM and 2–30 μM in lake water samples were obtained. The acceptable recoveries of 96.6 %-103.3 % indicated the high sensitivity and feasibility of the method. In conclusion, the prepared N-CDs are promising for Fe3+ detection in real environmental waters.

This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China [2018YFC1604204-3], the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province [KYCX22_2422] and the Key Research and Development Program of Jiangsu Province [No. BE2020756].
Data in this paper for academical purposes can be required from authors though Email.

Yan Zhou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Guoqing Chen: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Chaoqun Ma: Resources. Jiao Gu: Resources. Taiqun Yang: Validation. Lei Li: Supervision. Hui Gao: Data curation. Yi Xiong: Writing – review & editing. Yamin Wu: Data curation. Chun Zhu: Investigation. Hui Wu: Investigation. Wenzhi Yin: Visualization. Anqi Hu: Visualization. Xiaoqian Qiu: Visualization. Weinan Guan: Visualization. Wei Zhang: Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122414.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
